A Soaring Fund Shuns Japan and Mexico
Spectacular market losses in currency-battered Mexico and quake-shaken Japan have decimated many a foreign fund in recent months. Standing tall is GAM International.

 Its manager, John R. Horseman, adroitly dodged the danger zones, kept a significant cash cushion and larded his portfolio with European bonds, especially German ones. For the year, he has logged a total return of more than 9 percent, well ahead of the 7 percent loss for the average foreign stock fund. Although the surge follows a weak showing in 1994, his long-term record is stellar. For the five years ended in February, GAM International's annualized return exceeded 14 percent, adjusted for its 5 percent sales charge.

INVESTING IT;
How to Go Looking For Mr. Good Fund
INVESTING fads come and go, with everyone talking this summer about future technologies and computer stocks. Amid all the market chatter -- exchanged over cocktails, overheard on the train, whispered at museums -- people who have never put a dime in the stock market start to think about taking the plunge.

But tips on hot sectors do not make sound advice for a first purchase. Constrained by a small budget or restrained by limited financial experience, the beginner needs an all-weather investment, say a broad mutual fund, instead of a risky, high-flying technology stock.

Why Must Chrysler Be Driven Off a Cliff?
Kirk Kerkorian's attempted leveraged buyout of Chrysler appears to be losing steam. But his $22.8 billion hostile takeover bid and the rise in mergers and acquisitions activity this year shows that major pieces of our economic policy still need a drastic overhaul. 

As the merger mania of the 1980's first revealed, America's corporate structures, financial regulations and tax policies still pressure too many American businesses to sacrifice their long-term interests in pursuit of short-term profits.

Connecticut Q&A;: Jeremy Brecher;
What Is This Entity We Call Our State?
JEREMY BRECHER, of West Cornwall and New Haven, is a writer and historian who has spent most of his adult life listening to the stories of working people in Connecticut and sharing them through articles, books and public television documentaries.

"Strike!" published in 1972, has become a classic of American labor history. His other books include "Brass Valley: The Story of Working People's Lives and Struggles in an American Industrial Region,"written with Jerry Lombardi and Jan Stackhouse, and, most recently, "Global Village or Global Pillage: Economic Reconstruction from the Bottom Up," with Tim Costello.  Since 1989 Mr. Brecher has been humanities scholar in residence for Connecticut Public Television and Radio.

IN PERSON;
Painting the Future by Numbers
James W. Hughes is flailing his arms at the ceiling in a darkened classroom, invoking the God of Overhead Lights. The brand-new $42 million building known as Civic Square, half of which Dr. Hughes presides over, is so technologically advanced that the lights automatically turn off if they fail to detect motion after a sensible interval. Motion will turn them on again and, sure enough, Dr. Hughes's gyrations eventually produce a flicker of fluorescent light, then a steady flood.

Dr. Hughes, who has just become dean of the Edward J. Bloustein School of Planning and Public Policy at Rutgers, is clearly showing off. But who could blame him? This ultramodern five-story beige, red and black brick building on the edge of downtown New Brunswick ("a countercyclical capital infrastructure investment project that was instigated in the depths of the last recession," as Dr. Hughes diplomatically put it) is a major step up from the cramped office he used to occupy in Lucy Stone Hall. There, in his cinderblock cell, he studied demographic trends in the State of New Jersey and served as acting dean of the school for the past year.

For First-Time Buyers, a Silver Lining
WHEN Lee E. Koppelman, the executive director of the Long Island Regional Planning Board, and other planning officials from Long Island toured modular-house factories in Pennsylvania in March 1985, they were searching for answers to a growing problem -- the rising cost of housing that was driving out many of the region's young families.

"That's changed in a very dramatic way as a result of the recession, which we have not come out of yet," said Dr. Koppelman, who also is the director of the Center for Regional Policy Studies at the State University of New York in Stony Brook. "Housing is now a bargain -- but there aren't too many takers."

A Bargain Basement in Foreign Stocks
FOR people who hate to pay full price, mutual funds that invest abroad offer the closest thing to an end-of-winter sale.

While stocks in the United States surged in the first quarter, foreign stocks sagged. The damage was greatest in Mexico, where the devaluation of the peso left the stock market battered. Ripples from that crisis spread not only to other Latin American countries, but also to emerging markets around the world.

VOICES: VIEWPOINT;
Hands Off the S.B.A.! It's a Big Net Earner
THE Small Business Administration is one of many excellent programs under attack by the new Republican Congress. Some, like Senator Phil Gramm, the Texas Republican who is a Presidential contender, want to abolish it. But the logic for eliminating one of the most cost-effective programs in United States history is hard to find.

Where else can you get such a huge return on investment with almost no risk? For every dollar that the S.B.A.'s core lending plan, the 504 program, costs taxpayers, $200 is generated in private capital to finance new businesses and create jobs -- 350,000 of them since the program started in 1981. Meanwhile, 90 percent of S.B.A. loans stay current. The default rate is 1 percent.

If the New York State Department of Transportation recommends to Gov. George Pataki a deferral or "stretching out" of the reconstruction of the West Side Highway from the Battery to 59th Street, the downtown business community and the public would be betrayed -- again. 

Further delay would seriously undermine the commitments by the Governor and the Mayor to make the rebirth of lower Manhattan a major priority. It would also expose the state to the risk of having to return to the Federal Government the $450 million set aside for the project.

Who Should Worry About the Economy
All economic recoveries have their moments of doubt. That is the case today. The numbers have turned soft. They are highlighted by declines in industrial production, employment, home-building and car sales. The financial markets are wagering another bet that the end is at hand -- nothing new for an expansion that has received more than its fair share of disrespect. 

The conventional wisdom is that the Federal Reserve has little choice but to save the day with a cut in interest rates. So the bond market has rallied and long-term interest rates have plunged. But we have little to fear from the vagaries of the financial markets. A scant five months ago, the same bond players were convinced that the Fed would have to crunch a booming economy with sharply higher interest rates.
